glossary.def: The expected loss of a model over the entire data-generating distribution, not just the rows you observed. It is the quantity every validation scheme tries to estimate; you can only ever approximate it from a finite sample.

The cheapest validation scheme: split the data once into train and test, fit on train, score once on test. Unbiased for that particular trained model but high-variance — a single random split that can swing several accuracy points on luck alone.

Partition the data into k equal disjoint folds; in turn, each fold is the validation set while the other k-1 train the model. The mean of the k fold scores is the CV estimate, and their spread gives it error bars; k = 5 or 10 is the usual default.

k-fold that partitions within each class so every fold mirrors the overall label distribution. The default for classification and essential under class imbalance, where a blind random fold might contain few or no minority examples.

k-fold that keeps every group of related rows (same patient, video, or sensor) entirely on one side of each split. It prevents leakage in which a row and its sibling straddle the train/validation boundary and inflate the score.

Any path by which information from validation or test data influences training — duplicate or grouped rows split across the boundary, or preprocessing fit on the full dataset before splitting. It produces optimistic scores that collapse in production.

The rule for time-series cross-validation: every training timestamp must precede every validation timestamp in each fold (also called walk-forward or rolling-origin validation). Standard shuffled k-fold violates it and is invalid for temporally ordered data.

Two CV loops: an inner loop selects hyperparameters and an outer loop, whose folds the selection never touches, estimates performance. It removes the optimistic selection bias that arises when you tune and report on the same folds.

refs.cite: Stone, M. (1974). Cross-Validatory Choice and Assessment of Statistical Predictions. J. R. Stat. Soc. B 36(2).
Kohavi, R. (1995). A Study of Cross-Validation and Bootstrap for Accuracy Estimation and Model Selection. IJCAI 1995.
Varma, S. & Simon, R. (2006). Bias in Error Estimation When Using Cross-Validation for Model Selection. BMC Bioinformatics 7:91.
Bergmeir, C. & Benitez, J. M. (2012). On the Use of Cross-Validation for Time Series Predictor Evaluation. Information Sciences 191.
Arlot, S. & Celisse, A. (2010). A Survey of Cross-Validation Procedures for Model Selection. Statistics Surveys 4.
Cawley, G. C. & Talbot, N. L. C. (2010). On Over-fitting in Model Selection and Subsequent Selection Bias in Performance Evaluation. JMLR 11.
